meta.title: Marketing Story v5 (EN)
meta.author: Qzera
meta.description: Interactive marketing decision game: aligning marketing, dev, and strategy tracks for a Questell-based campaign.

default.label: Open reward
default.subtitle: External link — your campaign completion step.

choices.text: I open it
I’ll have one more coffee first

text.text: You’ve just put your morning coffee mug down on the desk when a new notification pops up: an email from the client, naturally marked as “URGENT”.

You give yourself half a minute of quiet with the coffee.
Now you’re ready to see what today’s “urgent” task is.

choices.text: I open the email
Time for a kick-off meeting
I go straight to the dev team
Let’s pause for a moment…

text.text:
The client wants an interactive, shareable, insight-driven campaign, live within a month and a half.

Extra request: every user should receive a unique visual outcome at the end.

In other words:
It has to be eye-catching, interesting, cost-efficient, and unlike anything you’ve done for them before — all on a tight timeline.

pages.title: Team meeting
pages.text:
(The screen is dark. A low hum. The meeting link is already open, but no one has spoken yet.)
Loading… then suddenly all cameras turn on at once, and everyone looks at you:
What’s the best way forward? What does everyone think?

choices.text: Tappable story format
Apester-style interactive quiz
Decision-based game

text.text:
Let’s start with the basics: how do we pull people in?
It has to be simple, work smoothly on mobile, and still feel like something the client hasn’t seen from us before.

choices.text: This is acceptable… but

text.text: {fragment:A1_storyly}
{fragment:A1_logic_puzzle}

choices.text: Use existing images
AI-generated, but unmoderated
AI-generated, with moderation

text.text: {fragment:A1_game}

choices.text: Prize draw
Everyone wins something
Experience-based reward

text.text: {fragment:A2_graphic}
{fragment:A2_ai_risky}
{fragment:A2_ai_safe}

choices.text: Let’s talk to the dev team
Let’s share what the devs said

text.text: {fragment:A3_draw}
{fragment:A3_reward}
{fragment:A3_ai_flow}

choices.text: Move to the conclusion

text.text:
You walk through all three directions with the dev team.
The only option that truly fits the client’s expectations is a bespoke game: experience, data, shareability — all in one.

choices.text: Think it through on your own

text.text:
In this exact form, you probably can’t deliver this quickly and cost-effectively in-house — at least not the way you’d like.
Okay, let’s find the version that will actually work.

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: You ask for their opinion right away
The team is a bit slow to start today
You’ve already talked to the team and have a draft concept

pages.text: You walk into the dev meeting with the fresh concept. The real question here: how do we build this in a stable, on-time way?

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: You explain why a quiz alone is not enough

pages.text:
You lay out the client needs clearly.
One of the devs doesn’t even look up:
“Why isn’t a normal quiz enough?”
The others nod — for them that’s the simplest option.

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: You ask about real cost and timeline

pages.text:
Finally someone looks up:
“Or… we could build an Interactive AI assistant.”
The rest latch on instantly — they start firing off technological possibilities in quick succession.

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: You reframe the need with clearer technical boundaries

pages.text:
The excitement dies down the second the numbers land: half a year of development and a budget the client will never approve.
It’s obvious: this is not the realistic solution right now.

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: You go back to the team to align

pages.text:
After a few rounds of back-and-forth, a middle-ground proposal emerges:
“Let’s build a bespoke game. It’s doable, predictable, and it will work.”
The team nods — technically, this is feasible.
You quickly play through how far this diverges from the client’s realistic budget.

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: Bespoke game, tailored to the brand
Template-based, fast-to-assemble version

pages.text:
The lead dev sums it up:
“We have two options: a bespoke game or an existing template.”
Bespoke is more flexible and more tailored.
The template is faster but more constrained — a compromise at every point.

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: You check the other option

pages.text:
The template-based version comes up: minimal customization options, and you’ve already used it for this brand several times.
You can already see the client’s face when they look at it.
This won’t land as the right solution.

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: You go back to the team to align

pages.text:
The bespoke development route sounds appealing — until the real time and cost appear.
The team is honest: this is platform-level work, not a pilot.
The client doesn’t have that kind of budget, and it wouldn’t be ready in time.
This is not the solution for now.

pages.title: Dev meeting

choices.text: You return to your team to align

pages.text:
The spec is clear: non-linear experience, stateful campaign engine, AI-based visual output.
Bespoke development would solve it, but the price is high, the timeline long, and the result single-use without heavy refactoring.
It needs a new approach — these proposals won’t work in the current frame.

choices.text: You go back to the dev team to clarify
You return to the team to align
You summarize the situation for yourself

text.text:
Based on the internal meetings, it’s clear what we’d actually need — but we can’t realistically build that right now.
Time to approach it from a different angle.

pages.title: Selecting key elements
pages.text:
Let’s start at the beginning. Think through what the client really needs.
Pick the 3 correct options.

text.default:
After a short round of research you find Questell: a platform built for non-linear, decision-based experiences that track user choices and generate personalized visual outcomes — quickly and cost-efficiently.

It’s exactly the kind of solution marketing can build on, while also reducing dev load.

text.text: {fragment:B_final_solution}

choices.text: On to the client
Let’s begin

text.text:
You sit down with the client for a short alignment.
“Okay. I’m listening. I just need one thing:
it has to be clear that this is a strong business decision, not just a flashy solution.”

They ask three questions.

pages.title: Client presentation

text.default: Client: “Let’s start with the most important part. Does the system make sure only brand-safe content appears? I don’t want AI visuals out there that don’t match the brand.”

text.text: The client is a bit impatient now: “Is the AI experience safe? No off-brand content can go out, right?”

pages.question: What’s the strongest business argument for a brand-safe AI experience?
options: Brand-safe AI visuals and moderation are built in.
We hope the AI won’t make mistakes.
If there’s a problem, we’ll just pull the campaign quickly.
pages.correctLabel: Exactly. Brand-safe AI visuals with moderation.

pages.title: Client presentation

text.default: Client: “Okay. And the experience itself? Will this be a truly interactive, decision-based flow — or will it end up behaving like a questionnaire?”

text.text: Same question again, only with raised eyebrows now.

pages.question: What do you highlight so it clearly feels like an experience, not a questionnaire?
options: Non-linear story, decision points, and a stateful system.
We’ll send an extra follow-up survey.
We’ll occasionally throw in random questions.
pages.correctLabel: Right: non-linear, stateful flow — not a plain survey.

pages.title: Client presentation

text.default: Client: “Last question. Do I get real insight from this? Not just click counts — I want to see how people think.”

text.text: They repeat the question, this time more slowly.

pages.question: What proves best that this generates real insight, not just numbers?
options: We only provide basic stats: how many people clicked.
Decision paths, drop-off points, and remarketing segments are built from interactions.
We leave insight up to the client’s gut feeling.
pages.correctLabel: Exactly. Decision paths, drop-offs, and remarketing segments = real insight.

choices.text: I’ll try again from the beginning

text.text:
The client leans back and quietly sighs. “That’s not enough. Think back to what was in the brief.”

The short silence says it all: you’ll have to start over — but at least now you know what doesn’t work.

pages.title: Finale
pages.text:
The campaign plan goes through.

The client nods, marketing is smiling, and the devs are not objecting.

The Questell-based, non-linear, stateful, AI-visual experience has been accepted.

Everyone is suddenly quiet — maybe because for once there is nothing to argue about.

The project doesn’t just work; it finally makes sense.

A1_storyly.text: The tappable story format is fast and visual, but engagement lasts just a few seconds. There’s no real decision space or story arc.

A1_logic_puzzle.text: Apester-style quizzes or polls are useful for quick feedback, but because they behave like questionnaires, they provide limited engagement and are not enough for a deep experience.

A1_game.text: A decision-based, game-like format is a strong solution: a short story, choices, feedback, and meaningful insight gathered along the way. Brand-tailored visuals and a strong balance of experience, measurability, and cost make it ideal for a launch campaign.

A2_graphic.text: With existing photos or stock images we simply can’t give every user their own visual; there aren’t enough variations available.

A2_ai_risky.text: Unmoderated AI visuals are flashy and fast, but unpredictable. A single bad generation can create serious legal and brand safety risk.

A2_ai_safe.text: Controlled AI visuals use predefined styles and an approval process, so you can generate consistent, personalized images quickly — without anything unacceptable going live.

A3_draw.text: A simple prize draw is quick, but it doesn’t build a real connection with the brand: people enter, submit, and move on. If there is a reward, it should be built into the experience itself, not just attached at the end.

A3_reward.text:
Giving every participant a reward is safe, but on its own it doesn’t necessarily drive deeper engagement.
It easily becomes a formality people claim quickly and then forget.

A3_ai_flow.text: The flow itself is the reward. When users feel their decisions matter, and the story responds to them, you gather insight without anyone complaining. It’s a rare win–win that makes a campaign meaningful in the long run.

A1_DONE.text: Marketing has already said, “it’s all a matter of creative decisions.”

B1_DONE.text: The devs have already said, “this is technically impossible — maybe one day.”

BA4_DONE.text: Dev alignment wrapped — feedback to marketing (path: BA4).

BB3_DONE.text: Dev alignment wrapped — feedback to marketing (path: BB3).

BC1_DONE.text: Dev alignment wrapped — feedback to marketing (path: BC1).

B_final_solution.text: This aligns with both the dev and marketing recommendations — probably for the first time in the project.
